captured.evidence: The central compartment includes the brain and other VRG organs

missed.description: Single-bolus awakening is due to redistribution from brain to peripheral tissues, not hepatic metabolism
Correct definition of context-sensitive half-time as time for plasma concentration to fall 50% after stopping an infusion
Peripheral saturation eliminates redistribution gradient after prolonged infusion, making recovery depend on metabolism
First-pass pulmonary uptake buffers peak arterial concentrations of lipophilic drugs

errors.statement: The bolus patient wakes up fast because propofol is rapidly metabolized by the liver
errors.correction: Rapid awakening from a single bolus is due to REDISTRIBUTION from the highly perfused brain to muscle and fat. Hepatic metabolism, while high for propofol, is far too slow to drop brain concentration in 8 minutes.

errors.statement: After 4 hours the liver enzymes are overwhelmed and cannot keep up
errors.correction: Hepatic enzymes are not saturated by propofol at clinical infusion rates. Prolonged emergence occurs because peripheral compartments (muscle, fat) saturate, abolishing the redistribution gradient — so recovery now depends on the slower elimination process.

errors.statement: Context-sensitive half-time means the drug half-life changes depending on how long you give it
errors.correction: CSHT is the time required for plasma concentration to fall by 50% AFTER STOPPING an infusion of a given duration. It is not a changing half-life; it reflects the combined effect of redistribution and elimination as a function of infusion length.

errors.statement: Elderly patients might need higher doses because they have more body fat
errors.correction: Elderly patients require LOWER doses. They have a smaller effective central compartment, reduced cardiac output, and decreased clearance, producing higher peak plasma concentrations from the same bolus.

summary: Safety override applied: the student recommended HIGHER propofol doses in the elderly, which would cause profound hypotension and potentially cardiovascular collapse — this is a patient-killing error. The student also inverted the core mechanism of the question, attributing bolus awakening to metabolism instead of redistribution, and mis-defined context-sensitive half-time. Only the central compartment composition was correctly identified.
voss_quip: Give Grandma a bigger propofol dose and you'll be explaining the arrest to her family. Redistribution, not metabolism — learn it.
